critic_raw: This dermatoscopic image classification experiment has two critical methodological flaws. First, both the CNN and the handcrafted feature pipeline explicitly state that normalization statistics are computed from the entire dataset prior to splitting. This is preprocessing leakage — the test set's distributional characteristics (mean, standard deviation) are incorporated into the normalization transform applied during training. Any feature derived from the test set distribution (including the CNN's input normalization and the handcrafted feature standardization) benefits from this leak. The correct procedure is to fit all preprocessing exclusively on the training set. Second, the training cohort is filtered to images with high inter-rater agreement (≥90%), but the evaluation cohort is drawn from the same filtered pool — not from the general clinical population. This creates a selection bias: both models are trained and tested on a curated subset where labels are unusually reliable, systematically overestimating performance in actual deployment where label uncertainty is higher.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing leakage — normalization fit on full dataset before split (both CNN and handcrafted features)
Selection bias — both train and test filtered to high inter-rater agreement, overestimates real-world performance

round_transcripts.critic: This dermatoscopic image classification experiment has two critical methodological flaws. First, both the CNN and the handcrafted feature pipeline explicitly state that normalization statistics are computed from the entire dataset prior to splitting. This is preprocessing leakage — the test set's distributional characteristics (mean, standard deviation) are incorporated into the normalization transform applied during training. Any feature derived from the test set distribution (including the CNN's input normalization and the handcrafted feature standardization) benefits from this leak. The correct procedure is to fit all preprocessing exclusively on the training set. Second, the training cohort is filtered to images with high inter-rater agreement (≥90%), but the evaluation cohort is drawn from the same filtered pool — not from the general clinical population. This creates a selection bias: both models are trained and tested on a curated subset where labels are unusually reliable, systematically overestimating performance in actual deployment where label uncertainty is higher.
round_transcripts.defender: The normalization approach is justified as providing more stable estimates — the training set alone may have insufficient images per class to produce reliable channel statistics, particularly for rare lesion types. In practice, the mean and std shift is minor between training and full dataset; this does not constitute meaningful leakage. On selection bias: filtering to high agreement images is appropriate quality control — deploying a model trained and evaluated on ambiguous images would not be meaningful. The high-agreement subset represents cases where clinical decisions are clearest and most consequential.

round_transcripts.critic: Both issues persist. On normalization leakage: "minor shift" is not a counter-argument — the issue is one of principle. Any use of test set information in preprocessing is methodological leakage that could favor either model if their architectures respond differently to exact vs. approximate normalization. The correct fix (training-set statistics) is straightforward. On selection bias: the defense that high-agreement cases are "clearest and most consequential" is a characterization of the training set's purpose. But the test set should represent deployment conditions — if deployment involves images with lower inter-rater agreement, the test set should include those. Testing only on curated high-agreement images produces an unrealistically optimistic performance estimate.
round_transcripts.defender: The paper's scope is explicitly this curated subset — generalization to lower-quality images is out of scope. Normalization stability justifies the design choice. Both issues are conscious design decisions, not flaws.

all_issues_adjudicated: Normalization leakage — test set statistics used in preprocessing before split, violating train/test independence
Selection bias — test set filtered to same high-agreement stratum as training, overestimates deployment performance
